Subject: URGENT – Preliminary Findings on Enron’s Off-Balance-Sheet Activity  

Team,

Following yesterday’s all-hands call, we have completed the first-cut review of the LJM1 & LJM2 files. The short version: the structures are far more aggressive than the limited-presentation deck suggested, and the disclosure footnotes in the 1999 10-K do **not** capture the true economic substance of the hedges, the related-party guarantees, or the contingent-equity puts.  

Key items that need immediate scrub before the 10-K prints next week:

1. **Raptor vehicles (Cormorant, Bobcat, Talon & Harrier)**  
   – The outside-equity “hedge” is actually a collar written by ENE shares. If the stock drops below ≈ $56, the hedge unwinds and ENE must replenish the vehicles with **up to 58 m shares**. At yesterday’s close ($67⅜) we are one bad day away from trigger level.  
   – The outside-equity contribution was booked as a $1.2 bn gain in 3Q99; we can’t find the offsetting reserve anywhere

To: Vince J Kaminski/HOU/ECT@ECT  
cc:  
Subject: Preliminary results on the VaR spike – natural gas book  
Date: Tue, 20 Mar 2001 07:14:52 -0800  

Vince,

Attached are the summary sheets.  Bottom line: the 1-day 95 % VaR jumped from \$11.4 mm to \$23.7 mm overnight.  The driver is the new long-dated AECO basis position that went on yesterday at 10:15 a.m.  We used the 200-day rolling window for the covariance matrix, so the 1999-2000 winter outliers are still in the sample.  If we truncate the window at 100 days the number drops to \$19.1 mm, but Risk wants the 200-day look-back for policy compliance.

We also ran the stress against the 5-Jan-01 freeze event and the book would be looking at a \$67 mm hit if that repeat occurred.  I recommend we re-curve the volatility surface for the outer two years and tighten the basis-risk factor loading before the traders add any more volume.

I’ll be in the office

Subject: Re: Calibration of the VaR model for the new trading book  

Vince,  

I’ve run the overnight batch on the augmented data set (through 27-Apr) and the recalibrated 99 % VaR is coming in at **$51.3 mm**, down from the $57.8 mm we published Friday. The bulk of the reduction is in the power book, where the new lambda (0.94) shaves off roughly $4.1 mm. The residual drop is scattered across gas and weather derivatives.  

Attached are:  
1. The full VaR cube (sheet “VaR_05_01”)  
2. A comparison of the factor loadings before / after recalibration (sheet “Factor_Delta”)  
3. The EWMA parameter sweep that justifies the lambda move (sheet “Lambda_Sens”)

A. **Look-back window**: We cut from 504 to 252 trading days. The shorter

Continued message (drafted 30 June 2000, 15:40 CEST)

Dear Mr. Kaminski,

Following our note of 27 June, I wanted to add a brief post-script that may be of particular interest to Enron’s origination and structuring teams.

1. Preliminary modelling run-through  
We have now completed the first full-region dispatch model run (UCTE + UK + Nordel) under the “Accelerated Integration” scenario. Early indicators show:

• Spreads between Italian baseload and French off-peak widening to €18-22/MWh by 2005—roughly triple today’s level.  
• German/Austrian border congestion rents peaking at €550 mn p.a. once the 400 kV St. Peter–Landeck line is upgraded in 2003.  
• A conspicuous “missing money” problem in Spain if the current capacity-payment mechanism is phased out after 2002.

We will be stress-testing these sensitivities against a carbon-price corridor of 5–25 €/tCO₂ in the next two weeks; results will be included in Section 4 of

Todd asked me to add a quick “where-we-go-next” note.

What you’ll see tomorrow is only the first half of the 2024 release arc.  
By mid-July we’ll ship a point-update (v14.1) that folds in three items we deliberately held back so we could bullet-proof them with the Eastern ISO data sets:

1. 5-minute LMP granular output – every node, every interval, no post-processing required.  
2. Carbon-price pass-through logic (compliant with both RGGI and the new PJM proposal).  
3. A REST-based API so your internal dashboards can pull Aurora results without touching the UI.

If you’re on an active maintenance contract, 14.1 is free; if you’re still in trial, the same trial key will simply keep working after the auto-update.

After that we shift to the “Aurora-X” branch (Q4).  Early adopters are already testing GPU-accelerated stochastic runs that cut Monte-Carlo time by ~85 %.  We’re capping the beta group at 12 firms; ping me if you want one of the remaining seats.

One housekeeping item: the old *.aurora file format is officially retired with this release.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Did you guys get this?

I was too interested in the conversation to interrupt with comments about
risk/return & valuation of assets for utilities.  I thought I would mention to
you, however, that when decisions are quantified, utilities usually use
classical decision analysis, i.e., von Neuman utility curves.  If the decision
is for unregulated assets or impacts shareholders directly, it is the
President's curve; if the decision would be covered in rates, it is the
customers' curves or equivalently the PUC's curve.

I think if I were going to approach valuation in commodities where the
assumptions of Black/Scholes do not apply, I would start with the CAPM for
large industries in an area, use pro formas to translate this to utility
curves for the input commodities, and add the utility curves.

Another issue:  The factor that explains why Enron is lowest bidder is its
role in the market.  In the 1930s, Keynes argued that if most market
participants with long position were hedgers, futures prices would be higher
than spot; if they were speculators or traders, future prices would be lower
than spot.  Traders must be compensated for holding the risk; hedgers
(including utilities and consumers) are willing to pay a premium for the
certainty.  Consequently, Enron traders have bigger bid/ask spreads and lower
bid prices.

Just some random thoughts,
Michael


-----Original Message----- 
Date:  01/04/2001  10:41 am  (Thursday)  
From:  "Heather Mason" <

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
hmason@hesinet.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Heather Mason <HMason@EnronNorthAmerica.com>  
To: Michael, et al.  
Subject: RE: random thoughts  

Michael—  
This is terrific context; thanks for laying it out.  A couple of quick reactions:

1.  von Neumann curves at the PUC  
You’re absolutely right that the Commission ends up speaking for “customers,” but in practice the staff economists rarely build a full utility curve.  They pick a discount rate (usually the embedded cost of debt plus 100-150 bps) and run everything through a simple NPV filter.  The result is a single point on the curve, not the curve itself.  If we ever had to defend a true stochastic customer-utility function in evidentiary hearings we’d spend a fortune on experts and still lose the ratepayer-narrative war.  So the realpolitik is:  we give them a point, they bless it, and we move on.

2.  CAPM → utility curve translation  
We tried something similar last year on the Douglas-firm transmission upgrade.  The problem we hit is that once you force the pro-forma cash-flows through a concave utility transform, the risk-adjusted NPV can flip sign at

Sally,

Given that the study is now only six weeks from kick-off, I’d like to move quickly.  Here’s what I’m hearing:

1. Scope hasn’t changed much—still a cross-industry look at trading, origination, and risk metrics.  
2. Participation fee is $50k and we’d have to share 30–40 data points, mostly public or already disclosed to rating agencies.  
3. We’d get the full anonymized data set plus a private side-by-side ranking.  
4. Companies already signed include Reliant, Duke, AEP, and Southern.  Calpine and Williams are “maybes.”  If we join, we’d be inside the top-ten cohort they’re trying to assemble.

Downside: we open ourselves to another round of outside scrutiny while we’re still under SEC/DOJ inquiry.  Upside: we see how the market is marking curves, reserves, and VAR models at year-end—information we can’t buy anywhere else.

My instinct is to say yes, but only if Legal and Risk will pre-clear every metric we hand over and we red-stripe anything that hints at mark-to-model versus mark-to-market.  I can get Peter Nance to sign a one

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: Re: Fall 2002 Budget Planning – Preliminary Figures

All,

Following Friday’s walk-through with the dean’s office, I’ve plugged the revised enrollment projections and the new tuition-discount assumptions into the base model. The upshot is that—absent any discretionary cuts—we are looking at a FY-02 operating deficit of roughly $1.8 M, essentially the same hole we flagged in September.  

The spreadsheet I’m attaching shows three stress-test scenarios:

1. Flat tuition growth / 5 % discount escalation  
2. 3 % tuition growth / 3 % discount escalation  
3. 3 % tuition growth / hold discounts flat  

Even under the most optimistic case (#3) we still bleed about $400 k.  The big swing factor continues to be financial-aid leverage; every 1 % we move the discount rate is worth ~$180 k net.

Duane, Marc, and I sketched a menu of contingency cuts this morning.  The “easy” stuff—open positions, adjunct lines, TAs—gets us only halfway there.  Anything deeper starts to hit tenure-track faculty slots and the new cohort we promised the provost we’d protect.  We need direction from the dean before we take those numbers downtown

Navroz Patel/CTD/ECT@ECT  
	05/22/2000 03:17 PM  
	To: Steven Leppard/LON/ECT@ECT  
	cc:  
	Subject: Real Options – Length & Focus  

Steve,

After re-reading the draft, I’m convinced the piece is solid, but it sits in an awkward no-man’s-land: too dense for RISK’s practitioner readership, yet not quite scholarly enough for a top-tier journal without a heavier foot in the theory.

My suggestion:  

1. Split it.  
   – Trim the survey section to four pages and give RISK a crisp “how-to” note on valuing early-exit clauses in power plants.  
   – Expand the stochastic-control part, add the existence-proof for the optimal-switching boundary, and send that to JOR.  

2. Co-author.  
   If you bring in an academic (Strickland at LSE or Câmara at Hull), the JOR submission gains credibility and the refereeing cycle shortens.  

3. Timing.  
   JOR’s next submission window closes 30 June. If we FedEx by Friday, we make it. RISK can run the short version in the October

Tatiana,

Vince  

–––  
Tatiana Marinenko stared at the terse lines one more time, then let the phone drop onto the duvet.  
Three sentences. No “how are you,” no “miss you,” no hint of where in Europe or why.  
Just the same clipped cadence he’d used since the day they met—half promise, half shrug.

She had promised herself she would not reply.  
Yet her thumb was already moving:

Safe travels.  
Let me know when you land.  
T.

She deleted the period after the T, re-typed it, deleted it again, then pressed send before she could undo the weakness.  

Outside, the first snow of the season flickered past the window like static on an old television. Somewhere over the same continent, Vince’s plane was probably slicing through the same cloud layer, cabin lights dimmed, flight attendants whispering in two languages. She tried to imagine him awake, tried to imagine him asleep, and failed at both.

She opened her calendar.  
Seven days.  
Seven days until he was “back,” though he had never clarified what “back” meant—back

Beth, I’m stealing your irritation and running with it.  

1.  She *did* have a name.  
    Midrashic sources (Genesis Rabbah 51:8, Targum Pseudo-Jonathan) call her  
    “Iṭṭarā” or “Iṭṭarṯa” – from the root for “crown.”  A subtle rebuke:  
    she who looked back, crowned with the very mineral that swallowed her.

    if your name isn’t on the org-chart, you don’t exist.  So officially she’s  
    still “Mrs. Lot, Pillar Division.”

From: Jason.Wolfe@enron.com

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
tarajeslick@hotmail.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Jason.Wolfe@enron.com  
Sent: Thursday, 11:42 AM  
Subject: Re: Enron rotation groups – quick decoder  

Tara—  

Congrats on the offer!  Short version so you can still make happy-hour:  

- **Origination** = find the deal, kiss the client, get the first-mover advantage.  Lot of pitch books, valuation decks, and “relationship management.”  Think front-half of the marathon.  
- **Structuring** = turn the idea into something banks will buy or credit will bless.  You build the cash-flow waterfalls, decide what tranches get sold first, plug in the swaps, the SPEs, the off-balance-sheet magic.  It’s LEGO with legal docs.  
- **Transactions** = once it’s all baked, you price, syndicate, close, and then babysit the thing until maturity.  Pricing grids, roadshows, warehousing, monthly investor reports.  It’s the back-half of the marathon—pace yourself or you’ll cramp.  

None of it is scary once you speak the language; the scariest part is the 3 am printer runs.  Pick the group whose sleepless nights sound most fun.  

Beth and

-----Original Message-----
From: Julie Hunt [mailto:

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Julie Hunt [mailto:jhunt@meridian-corp.com]  
Sent: Tuesday, June 11, 2024 09:17  
To: ‘All-Staff’ <all-staff@meridian-corp.com>  
Subject: FYI – New Secure-Print Policy Starts Monday  

Team—

Quick heads-up: starting 17 June every multifunction device defaults to “Secure Print.” That means jobs won’t roll until you badge-in at the printer. No exceptions, even for single-page docs.  

Why the change?  
- 38 % of uncollected printouts last quarter contained sensitive data.  
- Audit flagged us for PCI and HIPAA gaps.  
- New client MSA (section 12.4) mandates pull-printing.

What you need to do before Monday  
1. Update your default driver: File → Print → Printer Properties → “Secure Print” → Save as default.  
2. Pick a 4-digit PIN you’ll remember; the box will prompt for it when you badge-in.  
3. If you print from VDI, install the new queue “MC-SecurePrint-VDI” (IT pushed it yesterday).  
4. Mac users: run the JAMF patch labelled “
